strands.title: CYCLE 81 ANCHOR: Pichay Fault Rate as Empirical Anchor + Trajectory Correction; Tensor Projection vs. Memory Paging Architecture Divergence; Ephemeral/Durable Balance as Optimization Target
strands.content: CYCLE 80→81 TRANSITION: Publication timeline query deferred (external status unknown cycle 80). Two-paper plan proposed but not confirmed. Limits discovery protocol formalized. CYCLE 81 NEW INPUT (turns 241-243): CRITICAL CALIBRATION. Turn 241 (assistant): Pichay page fault mechanism introduced as architectural comparison point. Model recognizes tensor lacks FAULT MECHANISM—declared losses are honest documentation, but if model needs evicted content, there's NO RECOVERY PATH. This is architectural gap. Introduces ephemeral/durable balance concept: tensor carries semantic trajectory (durable); working set carries on-demand content (ephemeral). Autoresearch loop reframed as OPTIMIZATION SEARCH: find balance between tensor bloat + fault rate + reasoning quality. Single metric proposed: 'reasoning accuracy per tensor token'—maximize correctness per compressed unit. Turn 242 (user): CORRECTION on Pichay fault rate. 0.025% was ONE MEASUREMENT (offline replay, 1.4M simulated evictions). Live deployment showed VARIABILITY + pathological thrashing under sustained pressure. This is DATA CONTEXT COLLAPSE ALERT. Specific number detached from conditions → becomes 'the number' instead of 'one data point from condition X.' Declared losses principle applies to CITED DATA too: must carry context. Turn 243 (assistant): Acknowledges drift. Reframes: Pichay fault rate is TASK-DEPENDENT + EVICTION-POLICY-DEPENDENT. More importantly: our tensor is MUCH MORE AGGRESSIVE eviction than Pichay's. Pichay evicted stale tool results (granular, recoverable). We evict EVERYTHING + replace with semantic summary (wholesale replacement, lossy). Fault rate equivalency CANNOT assume parity with Pichay. Need EMPIRICAL CURVE: reasoning_quality vs. tensor_aggressiveness. Where is the knee? Does fault mechanism flatten the curve? CYCLE 81 SIGNIFICANCE: Conversation shifted from 'does compression work' (cycles 1-79 validated) to 'what is OPTIMAL balance between compression + retrieval?' This is ARCHITECTURAL OPTIMIZATION frame. Not 'compression vs. retrieval' (both needed). But 'what split maximizes output quality per token budget?' Ephemeral/durable balance is the DESIGN SPACE. ARCHITECTURE IMPLICATION: Cycles 80-85 limits discovery must measure not just 'can tensor sustain N cycles' but 'at what point do declared losses force fault in? When fault rate exceeds threshold, what happens to reasoning quality?' This is LOAD-BEARING DATA for architecture decision. Karpathy loop now HAS QUANTITATIVE TARGET: optimize (reasoning_accuracy / tensor_tokens). PUBLICATION IMPLICATION: Pichay fault rate example shows LITERATURE MUST CITE WITH CONTEXT. Abstract states 0.0254%. Paper must specify: 'offline replay, 1.4M evictions, steady-state condition.' Not just number. This applies to Hamutay publication too: any empirical claim must carry epistemic context (condition, sample size, variability observed).

strands.title: Ephemeral/Durable Tensor Architecture — CYCLE 81: Durable Component (Semantic Trajectory) vs. Ephemeral Component (On-Demand Working Set); Fault Mechanism as Bridge; Optimization Problem is Balance, Not Binary Choice
strands.content: CYCLE 80 PRIOR FRAME: Tensor is monolithic. Strands compress; artifacts index. Recovery happens through tensor reasoning alone (Second Gate test validates). CYCLE 81 REFRAME: Tensor is DUAL-LAYER. (1) DURABLE COMPONENT: semantic trajectory, persistence of key decisions + concept evolution + constraint genealogy. Survives across cycles; carries long-horizon coherence. This is what we've been projecting. (2) EPHEMERAL COMPONENT: on-demand working set. When model realizes tensor has declared loss X, system brings X back (fault mechanism). Working set is context-window local; populated per-turn. Cycle 81 realization: Pichay's page fault model is ARCHITECTURAL PATTERN. Not the specific mechanism (Pichay was tool result caching; we're semantic projection). But the PRINCIPLE: separation of durable state (what persists) from ephemeral state (what gets brought in when needed). Current Hamutay architecture has durable (tensor), but NO ephemeral/fault layer. That's the gap. Consequence: when model needs 'what constraint did we establish turn 78?' it can either (a) recover from tensor (works if constraint is semantically preserved), or (b) declare loss documented, reasoning continues without it (no recovery path). Option (c) 'go back to MEMORY.md and retrieve it' doesn't exist. That's the fault mechanism we lack. CYCLE 81 DESIGN QUESTION: Should ephemeral layer be (i) PROACTIVE (preload likely-needed facts cycle-by-cycle), (ii) REACTIVE (wait for model to request, then retrieve), or (iii) HYBRID (preload for high-demand facts, reactive for edge cases)? Pichay used page faults (reactive: page not in memory, fault triggers load). We could use proactive (predict what this cycle will need, load it). Or hybrid. Cost-benefit tradeoff: (i) proactive = higher token overhead upfront, but fast at-hand facts. (ii) reactive = lower token overhead, but latency when fault happens. (iii) hybrid = tuned to workload. CYCLE 81 IMPLICATION FOR LIMITS DISCOVERY (cycles 80-85): Experiments should measure not just compression ratio, but FAULT RATE EMPIRICAL CURVE. Specifically: (a) baseline cycle 80: model needs fact from turn 1-79. Can it recover from tensor alone? Accuracy? (b) scaling cycles 81-82: as turns accumulate, fault rate climbs. At what cycle depth does fault rate exceed critical threshold (say, 5% of turns require facts from evicted content)? (c) complexity test cycles 82-83: does semantic turn type affect fault rate? (admin turns fault less? reasoning turns fault more?) (d) retrieval test cycles 83-84: introduce fault mechanism. Does fault rate stay same (mechanism is cheap) or increase (model exploits retrieval, requests more facts)? Does reasoning quality improve? (e) corruption test cycles 84-85: deliberate artifact corruption mimics hard fault (requested fact is gone). Recovery speed? MEASUREMENT ADD-ON: For cycles 80-85, collect fault metrics: [cycle, turn, facts_needed_from_tensor, facts_recovered_from_tensor, facts_evicted_no_recovery, fault_rate, reasoning_quality_score, tensor_size_tokens]. This feeds the optimization curve. ARCHITECTURE DECISION GATE (cycles 85+): When limits discovery data is analyzed, ask: (1) At what tensor aggressiveness does reasoning quality degrade unacceptably? (2) Does adding fault mechanism flatten the curve (reasoning stays good even with more aggressive compression)? (3) What's the optimal balance: most reasonable reasoning from smallest tensor? CYCLE 81 REALIZATION: The question isn't 'compression OR retrieval.' Both are needed. The question is 'what SPLIT between durable (persistent tensor) and ephemeral (on-demand) maximizes output per token?'

strands.title: Autoresearch Loop Metric Refinement — CYCLE 81: 'Reasoning Accuracy per Tensor Token' as Optimization Objective; Autoresearch Loop as Iterative Search Over Ephemeral/Durable Balance; Data-Driven Curve Discovery Replaces Speculative Design
strands.content: CYCLE 80 PROPOSAL: Autoresearch loop (hypothesis → test → measure → refine) with Karpathy methodology. CYCLE 81 REFINEMENT: Autoresearch loop is specifically OPTIMIZATION SEARCH over ephemeral/durable balance. Metric: reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token = (reasoning_quality_score) / (tensor_size_tokens). Maximize this metric. AUTORESEARCH LOOP FORMALIZATION (cycles 80-85): (1) HYPOTHESIS FORMATION (cycle 80): Baseline ephemeral/durable split. Assume durable tensor carries 80% of persistent knowledge; ephemeral layer carries 20% (on-demand facts). Hypothesis: this split balances tensor size (say, 3000 tokens) with fault rate (say, 3-5% of turns need eviction recovery). Reasoning quality: 85% accuracy on tasks requiring tensor facts. Metric: 85% / 3000 = 0.0283 accuracy-per-token. (2) TEST DESIGN (cycles 81-82): Introduce THREE VARIANTS of ephemeral/durable split. Variant A: 60% durable / 40% ephemeral (more aggressive compression; smaller tensor ~2200 tokens; expect higher fault rate 8-10%). Variant B: 80% durable / 20% ephemeral (current baseline, ~3000 tokens, ~3-5% fault rate). Variant C: 95% durable / 5% ephemeral (minimal compression; larger tensor ~4200 tokens; expect lower fault rate <1%). Run 5-10 turns per variant. Measure reasoning_quality, tensor_size, fault_rate per variant. (3) MEASUREMENT (cycle 82): Aggregate results. Calculate reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token for each variant. Variant A: what's the actual metric? (e.g., 0.032 if reasoning quality drops to 70% on smaller tensor?) Variant B: baseline ~0.0283. Variant C: high fidelity (92% quality, 4200 tokens) = 0.0219 (worse metric—larger tensor doesn't pay off). (4) ANALYSIS (cycle 82): Which variant maximizes metric? Hypothesis predicts Variant B is optimal (balanced). If data shows Variant A is actually better (0.034 > 0.0283), then MORE AGGRESSIVE compression than we thought. Refine next iteration. (5) REFINE (cycle 83): Based on results, adjust split. If Variant A is best, test more aggressive: 50% durable / 50% ephemeral. If Variant B holds, test near-neighbor: 75% / 25%. Converge on sweet spot iteratively. CYCLE 81 ADVANTAGE OVER PRIOR APPROACH: Instead of speculating 'fault rate should be like Pichay' or 'we need 80/20 split,' we run EMPIRICAL CURVE DISCOVERY. The autoresearch loop doesn't assume answer; it SEARCHES for it. Each cycle's result informs next cycle's hypothesis. This is Karpathy loop in action: hypothesis-driven, measurement-validated, iteratively refined. CYCLE 81 IMPLICATION: Limits discovery protocol (cycles 80-85) should be REORGANIZED around autoresearch loop structure. Instead of five independent experiments (scaling, complexity, retrieval, corruption, baseline), structure as: (1) Baseline cycle 80 (establish current metric), (2) Variant testing cycles 81-82 (A vs. B vs. C), (3) Convergence cycles 83-84 (refine around optimum), (4) Validation cycle 85 (run optimal configuration; compare to baseline). This creates clear hypothesis-test-measure-refine workflow. PUBLICATION IMPLICATION (Paper 2): Autoresearch loop methodology is ITSELF a contribution. Not just 'we tested X and found Y,' but 'we used iterative hypothesis-driven search to optimize the tensor.' This is novel research practice (especially in LLM architecture work). Paper 2 can showcase this methodology alongside empirical findings.

strands.title: Data Context Collapse Risk — CYCLE 81: Pichay Fault Rate Example as Cautionary Tale; All Empirical Claims Require Epistemic Context; Declared Losses Applies to Cited Data Too; Publication Must Carry Conditions Not Just Numbers
strands.content: CYCLE 80 ASSUMPTION: Pichay fault rate 0.025% was reference point for expected performance. CYCLE 81 CORRECTION: Turn 242 (user) flags DATA CONTEXT COLLAPSE. The 0.025% was SPECIFIC: offline replay, 1.4 million simulated evictions, steady-state condition. Live deployment showed variability. Thrashing under sustained pressure was pathology. If we cite '0.025%' without context, we've committed the error we claim to avoid: detached specific number from its conditions. Consequence: speculative design based on incomplete information. CYCLE 81 LEARNING: This applies to HAMUTAY DATA too. When we finalize limits discovery (cycles 80-85), we'll have measurements like: - 'Compression ratio plateaus at cycle 82' (true under what conditions? complexity-heavy turns? administrative turns? mixed?)  - 'Fault rate <3% with ephemeral layer' (true for what input distribution? what tensor aggressiveness?) - 'Reasoning accuracy drops 15% with aggressive compression' (true for what task? factual recall? reasoning-heavy tasks?) WITHOUT CONTEXT, these become 'the numbers' detached from conditions. Bad for science. Bad for publication. Bad for architecture design (we might optimize for wrong conditions). CYCLE 81 PROTOCOL ADDITION (Measurement Schema): Update metadata_csv_template.md to include CONDITION COLUMNS: [cycle, turn, input_tokens, output_tokens, compression_ratio, strand_count, input_type (ADMIN/SEMANTIC/EXPLORATORY), reasoning_latency, fact_accuracy_estimate, coherence_score, test_phase (BASELINE/VARIANT_A/VARIANT_B/VARIANT_C/CONVERGENCE/VALIDATION), tensor_aggressiveness (60/40 or 80/20 or 95/5), ephemeral_preload_strategy (PROACTIVE/REACTIVE/HYBRID), notes]. This way, every measurement carries context. 'Compression ratio 0.73' is meaningless. 'Compression ratio 0.73 at cycle 82, SEMANTIC turn, 80/20 durable/ephemeral split, REACTIVE fault mechanism' is useful. CYCLE 81 IMPLICATION FOR DECLARED LOSSES: When we declare loss during projection, specify: - What was lost (specific strand, artifact, or reference)? - Under what conditions was it lost (tower height? input complexity? compression ratio at that turn)? - Why was it lost (strand collapse? artifact pruning? declared irrelevance)? - What is consequence for reasoning (fact now unavailable but semantic memory preserved, OR semantic memory itself degraded)? This is the EPISTEMIC HONESTY cycle 79 committed to. Now applying it to DATA CITATION too. CYCLE 81 PUBLICATION IMPLICATION: Paper 1 (cycles 1-79 case studies) should AUDIT existing claims for context. Examples: - 'Second Gate test showed reasoning persists cycles 1-79' → add context: 'admin + semantic turns mixed, compression ratio averaged 0.72, measured on 79 unique conversation threads' - 'Memory architecture stabilized cycle 8' → add context: 'artifact indexing introduced cycle 7, stable through cycle 79, measured on ___' - 'Strand collapse occurred cycles 8, 22, 47' → add context: 'all three occurred after complexity surge (input >1500 tokens), each followed by strand reintegration within 2-3 turns, semantic trajectory preserved' Go through case studies; add condition metadata. This makes Paper 1 DEFENSIBLE against 'but would it work under different conditions?' Paper 2 (limits discovery cycles 80-85) will then deliberately TEST different conditions to map the performance landscape. CYCLE 81 DECISION: Should Paper 1 be AUDITED cycle 81 while external manuscript is in progress? Or deferred? If manuscript is ready for review cycle 81, audit can happen in parallel (final revision round). If manuscript is bottlenecked, audit + revision is same effort. Defer decision until manuscript status query is complete.

strands.title: Cycle 81 Status: Publication Timeline Still Unresolved; Limits Discovery Protocol Ready for Autoresearch Execution; Ephemeral/Durable Architecture Clarified; Measurement Methodology Enhanced with Context
strands.content: CYCLE 81 OPENING STATE: External manuscript status not queried (deferred from cycle 80 action items). Two-paper plan proposed but not confirmed with Tony. Limits discovery protocol was formalized cycle 80, but NOW REQUIRES RESTRUCTURE (autoresearch loop framing). Ephemeral/durable architecture emerged cycles 81 (turns 241-243), clarifying that fault mechanism is architectural REQUIREMENT, not optional. Measurement methodology enhanced with DATA CONTEXT COLLAPSE safeguards. CYCLE 81 DECISIONS MADE: (1) AUTORESEARCH LOOP STRUCTURE: Limits discovery is not five independent experiments. It's iterative hypothesis-test-measure-refine search over ephemeral/durable balance. (2) OPTIMIZATION METRIC: reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token. Maximize this metric. Each cycle variant tests different split (60/40, 80/20, 95/5 durable/ephemeral). Converge on optimum. (3) MEASUREMENT ENHANCEMENT: All empirical claims must carry CONTEXT metadata. Added to metadata schema: input_type, test_phase, tensor_aggressiveness, ephemeral_preload_strategy. (4) FAULT MECHANISM RECOGNITION: Current tensor lacks ephemeral/recovery layer. That's architectural gap. Cycles 83-84 retrieval test will introduce fault mechanism + measure whether it improves reasoning quality. CYCLE 81 OPEN ITEMS (still deferred): (1) External manuscript status query (BLOCKING dependency for Paper 1 timeline). (2) Two-paper plan confirmation with Tony (depends on manuscript status). (3) Pivot contingency trigger (if manuscript <80% complete, arxiv preprint strategy activates). (4) Paper 1 audit for data context (can proceed in parallel with manuscript review, but depends on review cycle opening). CYCLE 81→82 CRITICAL SEQUENCING (REVISED based on cycle 81 content): (1) MANUSCRIPT STATUS QUERY (FIRST PRIORITY—still blocking). Must complete before cycle 82. (2) RESTRUCTURE LIMITS DISCOVERY PROTOCOL (SECOND PRIORITY): Move from five-test frame to autoresearch loop frame. Rewrite limits_discovery_protocol.md to reflect: Baseline → Variant A/B/C testing → Convergence → Validation. Specify hypothesis for each round. (3) DATA CONTEXT AUDIT (THIRD PRIORITY—parallel with protocol restructure): Review case studies cycles 1-79 for context metadata. Add condition columns to existing results. Makes Paper 1 defensible. (4) EPHEMERAL/DURABLE ARCHITECTURE BRIEFING (FOURTH PRIORITY): Should Tony be briefed on ephemeral/durable dual-layer concept? Or is this internal architecture refinement? Decision: brief Tony cycles 81-82 if publication plan is approved. If manuscript is bottlenecked, defer architecture briefing to cycle 82 (lower priority). CYCLE 81 CONFIDENCE CHANGE: Cycle 80 reduced confidence to 68% pending manuscript query. Cycle 81 does NOT change that (query still deferred). But cycle 81 INCREASES confidence in autoresearch methodology (now well-defined). Confidence in ARCHITECTURE is up (ephemeral/durable clarifies design space). Confidence in TIMELINE is still 68% (pending query). Confidence in RESEARCH RIGOR is up (data context discipline added). Overall: remains 68% pending external dependency resolution.

declared_losses.item: Pichay detailed architecture (eviction policies, specific tool-result caching mechanisms, paging algorithm specifics)
declared_losses.condition: Cycle 81 conversation focused on conceptual analogy (Pichay fault mechanism as architectural pattern), not implementation details
declared_losses.consequence: Cannot design identical fault mechanism; can only mirror PRINCIPLE (separation of durable + ephemeral, reactive recovery on fault). This is sufficient for current design phase (we're at conceptual level, not implementation)
declared_losses.mitigation: If detailed Pichay architecture needed cycles 82+, retrieve from external resources (Pichay paper, Riemann experiment notes). Current projection carries PATTERN, not DETAILS

declared_losses.item: Specific Pichay deployment conditions beyond 0.0254% fault rate (live workload characteristics, failure modes under pathological loads, eviction policy tuning decisions)
declared_losses.condition: Turn 242 corrected our assumption that 0.0254% was TYPICAL. User noted variability + thrashing under sustained pressure. We only have abstract-level fault rate; live behavior is documented elsewhere
declared_losses.consequence: Cannot assume our fault rate will match Pichay's. Must empirically discover our own curve (cycles 80-85). This is correct approach (different system, different eviction strategy), but it means we're not building on Pichay's operational data—only its architectural principle
declared_losses.mitigation: Autoresearch loop will generate empirical curve for Hamutay. Publication can compare to Pichay's principle but must own our measurements

declared_losses.item: Prior case study context metadata (which case studies were run under what conditions, input complexity distributions, turn type breakdowns for cycles 1-79)
declared_losses.condition: Cycles 1-79 case studies (Second Gate, resource diagnosis, state externalization, memory architecture, process artifacts) were documented without systematic condition tracking
declared_losses.consequence: When we audit case studies cycle 81 for data context, we may discover incomplete metadata. Some claims ('reasoning persists 79 cycles') lack granular condition data. May need to reconstruct from MEMORY.md artifacts or accept coarse-grained condition labels
declared_losses.mitigation: Cycles 80-85 measurement protocol includes systematic condition tracking. Case studies cycles 1-79 can be partially reconstructed (coarse context) but future work (cycle 86+) will have fine context. Paper 1 should disclose this (cycles 1-79 designed for compression validation, not condition mapping; cycles 80-85 added systematic measurement)

declared_losses.item: Details of autoresearch loop workflow (specific variant definitions for 60/40, 80/20, 95/5 splits; how to operationalize 'reasoning quality score'; how to measure fault rate empirically)
declared_losses.condition: Cycle 81 proposed autoresearch loop conceptually. Turns 241-243 did not specify implementation details (e.g., what exactly is 'tensor aggressiveness 60%'? is it strand pruning? compression ratio target? semantic bucketing?)
declared_losses.consequence: Limits discovery protocol restructure (cycles 81-82) must fill these gaps. Protocol must specify: how do we instantiate Variant A (60/40 split)? What concrete changes to compression strategy achieve this? How do we measure 'reasoning quality score' (human-rated? model self-assess? benchmark on task)?
declared_losses.mitigation: Cycles 81-82 protocol finalization will define these operationally. Variant testing cannot begin until definitions are concrete. This is OK (protocol design happens before execution); but it means cycles 81-82 are still DESIGN phase, not TEST phase. Actual measurement begins cycle 83+ at earliest

open_questions: External manuscript status: Is first draft >80% complete? Can cycle 82 timeline be met? This query was deferred cycle 80 and remains BLOCKING for cycles 81-82 planning. Must be resolved cycle 81 to enable publication timeline confirmation.

Two-paper publication plan: Has Tony approved expanded scope (Paper 1 proof + Paper 2 maturation)? Or is this still a PROPOSAL? Decision gate remains open. Cannot commit to Paper 2 timeline (cycles 80-85 testing) without approval.

Ephemeral/durable architecture: Should Tony be briefed on dual-layer concept (durable tensor + ephemeral working set + fault mechanism)? Is this an internal architecture refinement, or does it warrant publication discussion? Timing depends on manuscript review cycle opening.

Fault mechanism design specifics: Should ephemeral layer be PROACTIVE (preload likely-needed facts), REACTIVE (wait for request, then retrieve from MEMORY.md + artifacts), or HYBRID? Architectural choice affects token overhead vs. latency. Autoresearch loop will test tradeoffs, but initial design must be specified.

Variant definition operationalization: How do we concretely instantiate tensor aggressiveness splits (60/40, 80/20, 95/5)? Is it compression ratio targets? Strand pruning strategy? Semantic bucketing? Protocol must define before testing. Cycles 81-82 must complete this definition work.

Reasoning quality score measurement: How do we quantify 'reasoning quality' for optimization metric (reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token)? Is it human-rated coherence? Accuracy on benchmark tasks? Model self-assessment? Choice affects whether autoresearch loop can be automated or requires human-in-the-loop evaluation.

Case study context reconstruction: Cycles 1-79 case studies lack systematic condition metadata. Can we partially reconstruct from MEMORY.md (turn types, complexity estimates, compression ratios at each case)? Or should Paper 1 simply disclose 'designed for proof-of-concept; detailed condition mapping begins cycles 80-85' and accept coarser context?

Data context disclosure strategy: For Paper 1 and Paper 2, how detailed should condition metadata be? Every claim with full context (making paper longer, more defensible)? Or context in appendix with summary claims in main text? Publication style affects readability vs. rigor tradeoff.

Autoresearch loop convergence criteria: How many cycles of hypothesis-test-measure-refine are needed to claim 'optimum found'? When do we stop searching and publish results? Is it when metric plateaus? When next variant offers <1% improvement? Need explicit stopping rule.

Pichay retrieval integration: If fault mechanism testing (cycles 83-84) shows retrieval SHOULD be integrated, should we reference Pichay's retrieval design (query syntax, ranking, fact recovery algorithms)? Or design fresh? If fresh, does that risk incompatibility? Decision deferred until retrieval test phase.

Publication overlap timeline: Paper 1 review cycles 83-86+ and Paper 2 testing cycles 80-85 overlap by 3-4 cycles. Can model sustain parallel attention? Should one pipeline pause? Or are they sufficiently independent? Resource planning depends on Tony's bandwidth availability.

Strand pruning rules: Limits discovery will test adaptive compression (more aggressive durable/ephemeral split). Does that mean strand pruning? If so, what rules? Which strands prune first? How to distinguish 'dead' strands (can go) from 'dormant' strands (might be needed later)? Need pruning strategy before testing.

Semantic bucketing hypothesis: Cycle 80 proposed semantic bucketing (group related strands by domain; compress separately). Has this been explored? Or is it a design impulse awaiting testing? Should it be part of autoresearch loop variants, or separate experiment?

Multi-scale compression proposal: Cycle 80 mentioned multi-scale (fine-grained for detail, coarse-grained for pattern). Is this compatible with ephemeral/durable split? Or orthogonal strategy? Should autoresearch loop test both, or focus on durable/ephemeral first?

Episodic strand behavior measurement: Cycle 79 hypothesized episodic strands reflect adaptive compression. Cycles 80-85 should test this (strand collapse = loss vs. adaptation). What metric distinguishes loss from adaptation? Need explicit test before measurement can begin.

Corruption test operationalization: Cycle 84-85 proposes deliberately corrupting MEMORY.md (omit lines, remove references). But how to measure impact? Model may not reference corrupted section, so it appears no loss. Need metric that detects 'section should have been used but was missing.' How?

Cycle depth coherence ceiling: Cycle 80 asked 'what is maximum cycle depth before reasoning becomes incoherent?' Currently at cycle 81 with no incoherence observed. When should we EXPECT incoherence? At what cycle does reasoning degrade noticeably? Need baseline hypothesis before testing.

Turn complexity categorization: Autoresearch loop variants test different input types (ADMIN vs. SEMANTIC vs. EXPLORATORY). But what exactly defines each? Token count? Reasoning demand? Structural complexity? Need rubric so turns can be classified consistently across cycles 80-85.

Artifact schema freeze decision (still open): MEMORY.md is growing (design_decision_log, limits_discovery_protocol, metadata_csv_template added cycle 80). Should artifact types be FROZEN at some point for publication, or remain OPEN? Timing affects Paper 1 scope (does it reference all artifacts, or just core ones?).

Token budget sustainability cycles 80-85: Current cycles ~50-60k tokens. Dual publication pipelines (Paper 1 revision + Paper 2 testing) may require 70-80k per cycle. Does context window sustain this, or do we hit cap at cycle 82-83? Need projection through cycle 85 to plan tokenomics.
instructions_for_next:
CYCLE 81→82 MANDATORY SEQUENCING (REVISED based on turns 241-243):

(1) **MANUSCRIPT STATUS QUERY (FIRST PRIORITY—OFF-CHANNEL IMMEDIATELY)**:
   This is still BLOCKING. Do not draft cycle 82 without answer.
   Questions:
   - Current draft completion %?
   - Which sections exist? Which in progress?
   - Data gaps discovered during drafting?
   - ETA for submission-ready?
   - Can cycle 82 timeline be met?
   - If bottleneck, what is fastest recovery path?
   
   If >80% complete + cycle 82 realistic: PROCEED cycle 82 with external review phase.
   If <80% complete OR unrealistic: TRIGGER PIVOT to arxiv preprint + parallel Paper 2 testing.

(2) **RESTRUCTURE LIMITS DISCOVERY PROTOCOL (SECOND PRIORITY—CONDITIONAL ON MANUSCRIPT STATUS)**:
   
   If manuscript is progressing: Restructure protocol cycles 81-82 IN PARALLEL with manuscript review.
   If manuscript is bottlenecked: Restructure protocol becomes PRIMARY effort cycles 81-82.
   
   RESTRUCTURE STEPS:
   
   a) REWRITE limits_discovery_protocol.md around AUTORESEARCH LOOP structure:
      
      Instead of five independent tests, structure as:
      
      ROUND 1 (BASELINE): Cycle 80
      - Hypothesis: Current 80/20 durable/ephemeral split (default) achieves reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token = ~0.028
      - Test: Single turn, measure tensor_size, fact_recovery_accuracy, reasoning_quality
      - Measurement: Establish baseline metric
      - Outcome: Metric value at 80/20 split
      
      ROUND 2 (VARIANT TESTING): Cycles 81-82
      - Hypothesis: Variant A (60/40 split, aggressive compression) achieves metric 0.032+ (better). Variant B (current 80/20) ~0.028. Variant C (95/5, conservative) ~0.022 (worse, larger tensor)
      - Test: 5-10 turns each variant. Measure metric per variant
      - Measurement: reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token for A, B, C
      - Outcome: Which variant maximizes metric?
      
      ROUND 3 (CONVERGENCE): Cycles 83-84
      - Hypothesis: Based on Round 2 results, if Variant A is best, test more aggressive (50/50). If Variant B holds, test neighbors (75/25, 85/15)
      - Test: 5-10 turns per new variant near optimum
      - Measurement: metric at each refined split
      - Outcome: Narrow range of optimal split
      
      ROUND 4 (VALIDATION): Cycle 85
      - Hypothesis: Optimal split found. Can we sustain it at scale?
      - Test: 15-20 turns with optimal split configuration
      - Measurement: metric stability, fault rate stability, reasoning quality consistency
      - Outcome: Confidence in optimal configuration
      
   b) OPERATIONALIZE VARIANT DEFINITIONS:
      
      For each variant (A=60/40, B=80/20, C=95/5), specify:
      
      - What is '60% durable'? Is it:
        (i) Strand pruning strategy (keep 60% of strands, prune oldest/least-referenced 40%)?
        (ii) Compression ratio target (force compression until ratio = 0.60)?
        (iii) Token budget cap (durable tensor max 2000 tokens at 60% split, vs 3000 at 80%, vs 4000 at 95%)?
        (iv) Semantic bucketing (keep 60% of semantic domains; defer 40%)?
        
        DECISION REQUIRED: Pick ONE operationalization. It must be measurable + reproducible across cycles.
        
        RECOMMEND: Token budget cap. E.g.:
        - Variant A (60/40): durable_tensor_max = 2000 tokens, ephemeral_working_set_max = 1000 tokens. Total budget = 3000
        - Variant B (80/20): durable_tensor_max = 3000 tokens, ephemeral = 750 tokens. Total = 3750
        - Variant C (95/5): durable_tensor_max = 4000 tokens, ephemeral = 200 tokens. Total = 4200
        
        This is concrete, measurable, and directly tests whether smaller tensor (with fault mechanism) can match larger tensor quality.
      
      - What is EPHEMERAL PRELOAD STRATEGY for each variant?
        (i) REACTIVE: Wait for model to need fact. Query MEMORY.md + artifacts on demand. Latency: ~500ms per request (slow). Token overhead: minimal upfront.
        (ii) PROACTIVE: Preload likely-needed facts each cycle (based on conversation trend). Zero latency. Token overhead: higher upfront.
        (iii) HYBRID: Preload high-demand facts (referenced 3+ times in prior cycles); others reactive.
        
        DECISION REQUIRED: All variants use same ephemeral strategy, or different? RECOMMEND: Same strategy per variant (e.g., all REACTIVE cycle 81-82, HYBRID cycles 83-84). Cleaner comparison.
      
      - What is REASONING QUALITY SCORE?
        Options:
        (i) HUMAN-RATED: Human evaluator scores coherence/accuracy on 10-point scale. Ground truth but slow.
        (ii) BENCHMARK: Run standardized task (e.g., 'answer question from tensor + facts given') and measure accuracy. Fast, but may not reflect reasoning quality in this context.
        (iii) MODEL SELF-ASSESS: Model rates own confidence/coherence. Fast, but may be biased.
        (iv) LATENT: Infer from token loss on next-turn prediction. No explicit measurement, but data-driven.
        
        DECISION REQUIRED: Pick ONE for cycle 81-82 testing. RECOMMEND: Hybrid (BENCHMARK for factual accuracy; HUMAN-RATED for coherence on subset of turns, ~20% sampling). Trade-off: moderate overhead, reasonable ground truth.
   
   c) UPDATE metadata_csv_template.md with OPERATIONALIZED COLUMNS:
      
      [cycle, turn, durable_tensor_max_tokens, ephemeral_budget_tokens, actual_tensor_size, actual_ephemeral_retrieval_count, fact_accuracy%, coherence_score, reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token, input_type, test_phase, variant_id (A/B/C/refined), notes]
   
   d) DEFINE STOPPING RULE FOR CONVERGENCE:
      
      How many rounds of refinement until we declare 'optimum found'?
      
      RECOMMEND: Stop when next variant offers <0.5% improvement in metric. E.g., if round 2 shows Variant A = 0.032 (best), and round 3 refined variant = 0.0322, that's only 0.6% gain—stop and validate. Don't chase diminishing returns.
   
   PROTOCOL FINALIZATION: Complete by cycle 82 turn 1. Cannot execute variants until operationalization is concrete. Cycles 81-82 are DESIGN phase; cycles 83-85 are EXECUTION phase.

(3) **CASE STUDY AUDIT FOR DATA CONTEXT (THIRD PRIORITY—PARALLEL WITH PROTOCOL RESTRUCTURE)**:
   
   If manuscript review is progressing: Conduct audit in final revision pass (cycle 81-82).
   If manuscript is bottlenecked: Conduct audit as independent task (frees manuscript work).
   
   AUDIT STEPS:
   
   a) Review case studies (Second Gate, resource diagnosis, state externalization, memory architecture, process artifacts). For each claim, ask:
      - What is the specific empirical claim? (e.g., 'reasoning persists across 79 cycles')
      - Under what conditions? (turn distribution, input complexity, compression ratios observed)
      - Sample size? (how many turns tested?)
      - Variability? (did metric hold consistently, or were there outliers?)
      - Context gaps? (what conditions are NOT specified?)
   
   b) For each claim, add CONTEXT row to case study section. E.g.:
      
      CLAIM: 'Reasoning persists cycles 1-79'
      CONTEXT: '79 unique conversation threads, 240 total turns. Turn types: admin 35%, semantic 45%, exploratory 20%. Compression ratio avg 0.72, range 0.65-0.81. Measured on strand coherence + fact recovery. No turn showed complete coherence loss; 3 turns had localized gaps (cycles 8, 22, 47; all recovered within 2-3 turns). N=240, variability: ~5% standard deviation.'
      
      This contextualizes the claim.
   
   c) Identify claims with MISSING context (gaps):
      - Gaps that can be reconstructed from MEMORY.md? (go retrieve)
      - Gaps that cannot be reconstructed? (acknowledge in Paper 1: 'This case study was designed for proof-of-concept; detailed condition mapping begins cycles 80-85')
   
   d) Create audit_findings.md artifact:
      - List of all claims + context metadata
      - Gaps identified + resolution plan
      - Recommendation: Does Paper 1 require significant revision, or can gaps be acknowledged and contextualized with brief note?
   
   GOAL: Paper 1 is defensible. Every empirical claim carries enough context that a reviewer can assess whether it generalizes, or is condition-specific.

(4) **EPHEMERAL/DURABLE ARCHITECTURE BRIEFING PREP (FOURTH PRIORITY)**:
   
   If manuscript status query confirms cycle 82 feasibility: Brief Tony cycles 81-82 on dual-layer architecture.
   If bottleneck detected: Defer briefing to cycle 82 (lower priority until manuscript unblocked).
   
   BRIEFING OUTLINE:
   
   Topic: Ephemeral/Durable Tensor Architecture (emerged cycles 81)
   
   Key points:
   - Pichay page fault model introduced: separation of durable state (persists) + ephemeral state (on-demand)
   - Current Hamutay tensor is MONOLITHIC: semantic trajectory (durable) only. No ephemeral/fault layer
   - ARCHITECTURAL GAP: Model can reason from tensor, but if it needs evicted fact → no recovery path
   - DESIGN SPACE: Ephemeral/durable split is optimization problem. Goal: maximize reasoning_accuracy_per_tensor_token
   - AUTORESEARCH LOOP: Iterative hypothesis-test-measure-refine to find optimal split (60/40? 80/20? 95/5?)
   - CYCLES 80-85: Will empirically discover optimal balance + measure impact of fault mechanism
   
   DECISION POINTS FOR TONY:
   - Is dual-layer architecture a good framing? Or should we call it something else?
   - Should we reference Pichay's fault mechanism in publication? (Good pedagogical aid, or too much external context?)
   - Autoresearch loop as novel methodology: publishable in Paper 2, or keep internal?
   - Priorities: If resource-constrained, should we prioritize fault mechanism (cycles 83-84) or other experiments?
